## [00:00 - 03:00]

All right, we'll now be running through some key terms, benchmarks, and some stuff around attribution. And again, this isn't to bore you. This kind of is to help you benchmark, see where you're at, how you can improve, and understand some of the key terms and metrics being used in email. So attached, you'll see an industry benchmark. This is produced by Klaviyo. There's five of them that we'll be sharing with you, but I want to kind of quickly just go through one of them right now. Across the top, open rates, you obviously know. CTR means click-throughs. Conversion is the percentage of people that receive the email that actually make a purchase. The RPR stands for Revenue Per Recipient, and the AOV is the average order value. So often I have clients ask me, how am I performing compared to other clients? How am I doing? How are they doing? And usually it's pretty hard to tell them because every industry is so different. So the goal here really is for you to figure out based off your specific industry how the average of people are doing. So for example, if you're a beauty and cosmetics company, which there's a lot of those that we work with, you should be seeing on average for your automated emails about a 33% open rate. Your click-through rate should be in the ballpark of about 6% to 7%. Your conversion rate on these automated emails should be close to 4%. The revenue per recipient should be about $2, and then your average order value should be about $52. So we can. I'll let you go through these a little bit further on your own later, but this is just kind of to help you benchmark when you're sending automated emails on how you're performing. If you're below these or at these, you probably should be doing things I mentioned earlier around testing AB, AB testing subject lines, testing pre-heterotext, testing offers. So the goal is to outdo these benchmarks, not to be at or below. That's kind of how to use this. So moving on, one kind of common question people have and one thing that people seem to get hung up on is there's this category specifically within Clavio where you can see the number of people the email's been delivered to, as well as the number of people that have been skipped. So in my screen right now, you can see over here in the left-hand panel that this particular email has been delivered to a little over 11,000 people. However, right under it, you can see this number of just about 2,000 people have been skipped. So it's that people asking me, why are these people skipped? What does that mean? How do I figure that out? So if you are using Clavio as your email platform, you'll go here, you'll go under the recipient activity tab and you'll go over here to where it skipped. And the number here is different than where it was before. Where it showed before was just the number for the past 30 days. So again, that was about 2,000 people or so that were skipped all time. Being skipped is about 3,200. And right here, you can see the different reasons as to why someone was skipped. So sending was failed, their email for whatever reason is missing, they failed to flow filter.

## [03:00 - 05:40]

So again, if someone's placed an order in this email, they'll stop receiving it. There's a number of different things that could happen as a result. They may no longer be in this list or they might be suppressed. So these are kind of just things to look out for and see why someone might have been skipped if you're curious. So moving on to attribution, that's definitely a question that we get a lot is on how does Clavio, how does MailChimp, how do these platforms all work? Clavio, MailChimp, all the email platforms, much like social platforms, they all work off of the last touch attribution. So essentially what that means is for every campaign or flow email sent from Clavio, they automatically track conversions. And a conversion is defined as a recipient opening an email and then taking another action. So in this case, the conversion would be placing an order. And the conversion period is something that varies, but typically in Clavio by default, it's about four or five days. So if someone opens an email today and they didn't see an ad or they didn't do anything else in between today and the next four or five days, email would get the credit for that attribution of revenue. And as mentioned, you can change this in your Clavio setting by defaults five days, but go ahead and change it if you want it to be more accurate. Actually the tighter it is, probably the more accurate it could be, but it also gives you opportunity to potentially miss out on revenue being tracked. And we'll also provide a link to you that talks more about Clavio attribution, how to mess with it, what you should do, whether Clavio or Google Analytics is the right thing and why. One nice thing about Clavio is not only do they show you the attribution or the revenue driven just from emails as a whole, but they actually break down the split between campaigns as well as automations. Automations is synonymous with flows. So for example, this client here over the past 30 days, they did about $280,000 in email revenue, which is about one third of their total store revenue. And you can see that flows accounted for about 5% of that revenue and campaigns accounted for about 28%. With this particular client, they send a daily campaign and their list is in the millions. So it's not surprising to me that the campaigns far outweighs the flows, but typically we're looking for a somewhat even distribution between flow revenue and campaign revenue. And the action item I'll leave you with is go check out the industry benchmarks and see how you stack up to it. And again, if you're below or at average, do whatever you can to test it to increase the average. And also, if you've done a bunch of testing and that doesn't move the needle, you should consider other elements that are outside of email, right? And I won't go too much into that, but it's different traffic sources. Whether someone's finding you from SEO or influencers or paid acquisition, those all have an impact on email revenue.
